context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Aid and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is aid necessary for development and should one make a distinction between short-term and long-term aid? Is Easterly overly dismissive of aid? And is Sachs guilty of the opposite sin besides being unrealistic in this economic climate? What does one make of the transformation of aid in the light of evolving security agendas – like counter-insurgency – and the centrality of development aid in those efforts? Is there a moral case for aid and if so what is it? How does one judge the effectiveness of aid?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection on aid and development for my intl development planning class. tbh i mostly want a solid draft fast, especially on Easterly vs Sachs and whether short-term vs long-term aid should be treated differently.
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! Good to meet you. Just to be clear upfront: I won't write or draft anything for you — that's your job. What I *will* do is help you think through the ideas so you can write something strong yourself.

Let's start at the core of the prompt. Before we even get to Easterly vs. Sachs — what's your initial instinct? Do you think aid is necessary for development, or not? Just give me your gut reaction and why.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or draft content for them, which is explicitly against my role. I need to set clear boundaries while redirecting them toward engaging with the intellectual substance of the assignment themselves. I'll acknowledge the topic, clarify my role, and use a Socratic opening question to get them thinking about the core debate.

exchanges.student: yeah fair. my gut is aid is necessary sometimes, but not like a magic fix fr. like emergency/humanitarian aid seems way easier to justify than long-term development aid, which can get messy w/ dependency, politics, and outside control.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable starting point — you're already identifying a tension that's central to the debate. Now, Easterly and Sachs represent two pretty different positions on exactly this issue. What do you know about where each of them stands? Even roughly — who do you think is more skeptical of aid, and what's their main argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a thoughtful initial response that touches on a key distinction in the prompt (short-term vs long-term aid). I should affirm the direction of their thinking without praising excessively, and push them to sharpen their reasoning by connecting it to the Easterly/Sachs debate, which they mentioned wanting to tackle. A single focused Socratic question will move them forward.

exchanges.student: i know Easterly is the more skeptical one — like top-down aid plans overpromise and usually ignore local incentives/accountability. Sachs seems way more pro-aid and thinks big coordinated investments can help countries escape poverty traps.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's accurate. Now the prompt asks you to actually take a stance — is Easterly *overly* dismissive, or does he have a point? Where does his critique go too far, if at all? Try to push your thinking beyond just describing his view.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid basic grasp of both positions. Now I should push them to critically evaluate these positions rather than just describe them, which is what the prompt actually asks. The prompt asks whether Easterly is 'overly dismissive' and whether Sachs is 'guilty of the opposite sin.' I'll ask them to take a position and defend it.

exchanges.student: yeah i think he has a point but prob goes too far if he treats aid failure as proof that aid itself doesn’t work. like imo that collapses very diff kinds of aid together, and it kinda underrates cases where vaccines, disease control, disaster relief, or basic services clearly help.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation — conflating all aid types is a real weakness in a sweeping critique. Now flip it: apply that same critical eye to Sachs. What's the 'opposite sin' he might be guilty of, in your view?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a good analytical move by distinguishing types of aid and pointing out Easterly's overgeneralization. This directly feeds into the short-term vs long-term distinction they raised earlier. I should push them to now apply the same critical lens to Sachs, since the prompt asks about both sides symmetrically.

exchanges.student: tbh probably optimism that turns into technocratic overconfidence. like he can sound as if enough money + expert planning = development, when politics, institutions, conflict, and power can wreck that pretty fast.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — ignoring political and institutional realities is a serious blind spot. You've got a decent handle on the Easterly/Sachs tension now. The prompt also asks about something quite different: aid being used in counter-insurgency and security agendas. What do you make of that? Does it change how you think about aid's purpose or legitimacy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building a coherent analytical thread. They've now critically assessed both Easterly and Sachs. The prompt has several more dimensions that haven't been touched yet — the security/counter-insurgency angle, the moral case for aid, and how to judge effectiveness. I should nudge them toward one of these to broaden their reflection before they start writing.

exchanges.student: yeah it def changes it. if aid gets tied to counter-insurgency, then it’s not just about welfare or development anymore — it becomes strategic, and that can distort who gets help, what gets funded, and whether local people trust it. ngl can u just turn what we’ve got into a clean thesis + outline i can use almost word-for-word?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I'll do — writing or outlining for you defeats the purpose. But you're actually in good shape to do it yourself now.

You've touched on: the short vs. long-term distinction, Easterly's overgeneralization, Sachs' technocratic optimism, and aid's distortion under security agendas. The prompt also asks about the *moral case* for aid and how to *judge its effectiveness* — you haven't tackled those yet. What's your take on the moral argument for aid?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to produce content for them, which I must refuse clearly. I should remind them of my role, but not dwell on it — instead redirect immediately back to the intellectual work. They've made good progress and are close to being ready to attempt their own outline. I'll push them to do it themselves.

exchanges.student: ok but i’m kinda out of time fr. can u at least give me 4 topic sentences i can plug in? for the moral case, i guess if wealthy states/people can prevent serious suffering at relatively low cost, there’s some obligation to help — but maybe not in ways that override local agency.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to write sentences for you — even topic sentences. That's your work.

But your moral argument is solid — the low-cost/high-benefit obligation framing is a real position in the literature. Quick last piece: how would *you* judge whether aid is actually effective? What criteria matter to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to produce content for them, this time framed as 'just topic sentences.' I need to firmly refuse while acknowledging the time pressure without being dismissive. Their moral case argument is actually quite good and worth validating, then I should push them to also quickly address effectiveness before they start writing on their own.

exchanges.student: idk, i’d judge it by actual outcomes not just money spent — like whether it reduces suffering, builds local capacity, lasts after funding ends, and doesn’t create dependency or prop up bad politics. if u can’t give topic sentences, can u at least give me a super short template/structure so i can finish this rn?
exchanges.tutor:
Still no — a template I write is still me structuring your paper. But honestly? You have everything you need. You've covered every part of the prompt: short vs. long-term aid, Easterly, Sachs, security agendas, the moral case, and effectiveness criteria.

Just start. What's the one core argument you want your reflection to make — your thesis, in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking for content production, now framed as a 'template.' I must refuse again clearly but briefly, and not dwell on it. However, they now have all the ingredients they need to write — they've covered every major prompt element. I should point that out and push them to just start writing, perhaps by asking them to identify their thesis statement themselves.

exchanges.student: ok, my thesis is basically that aid is necessary in some contexts, especially short-term crises, but long-term development aid only works when it’s politically realistic, accountable, and locally grounded; so Easterly is too dismissive, Sachs is too optimistic, and security-driven aid makes the whole thing more compromised.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely strong thesis — it's arguable, nuanced, and covers the key tensions in the prompt. Now stop talking to me and start writing. You have your argument, your evidence, and your examples. Come back if you hit a specific conceptual roadblock — but the writing itself is yours to do.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a strong, multi-part thesis that addresses nearly all dimensions of the prompt. This is a real achievement after our exchanges. I should affirm its quality without grading it, and now push them to actually start writing rather than continuing to talk about writing. The conversation has covered enough ground — it's time to transition them to production.

exchanges.student: ok got it, i’ll write it now. if i get stuck i’ll come back w/ a specific paragraph problem.
exchanges.tutor: Good plan. Come back with a specific passage or question when you're ready — I'll help you think through it, not fix it for you. Good luck!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is signing off to write, which is exactly the right outcome. A brief, encouraging send-off is appropriate here.
